From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American rock singer (born 1945)

"Mitch Ryder & the Detroit Wheels" redirects here. For Mitch Ryder's backup band, see [The Detroit Wheels](/wiki/The_Detroit_Wheels "The Detroit Wheels").

Musical artist

**William Sherille Levise Jr.** (born February 26, 1945), known professionally as **Mitch Ryder**, is an American rock singer who has recorded more than 25 albums over more than four decades.[[1]](#cite_note-The_Ryder_Stipulates-1)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mitch_Ryder&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Career")]

Ryder was born on February 26, 1945, in [Hamtramck, Michigan](/wiki/Hamtramck,_Michigan "Hamtramck, Michigan").[[2]](#cite_note-2) He spent his high school years in [Warren, Michigan](/wiki/Warren,_Michigan "Warren, Michigan"), a suburb north of [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit").[[3]](#cite_note-3) He formed his first band, Tempest, when he was in high school, and the group gained some notice playing at a Detroit [soul music](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") club called The Village.[[4]](#cite_note-Mitch_Ryder_Biography-4) Ryder next appeared fronting a band named Billy Lee & The Rivieras, which had limited success until they met songwriter / record producer [Bob Crewe](/wiki/Bob_Crewe "Bob Crewe").[[4]](#cite_note-Mitch_Ryder_Biography-4) He selected his stage name when he saw "Mitch Ryder" in the [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") telephone directory and renamed the group Mitch Ryder & [The Detroit Wheels](/wiki/The_Detroit_Wheels "The Detroit Wheels").[[5]](#cite_note-5) They recorded several hit records for his [DynoVoice Records](/wiki/DynoVoice_Records "DynoVoice Records") and New Voice labels in the mid to late 1960s, most notably 1966's "[Devil with a Blue Dress On](/wiki/Devil_with_a_Blue_Dress_On "Devil with a Blue Dress On")", their highest-charting single at number four, as well as "Jenny Take a Ride!" which reached number 10 in 1965, and "Sock It to Me, Baby!", a number six hit in 1967.[[6]](#cite_note-Larkin-6) The Detroit Wheels were John Badanjek on drums, Mark Manko on lead guitar, Joe Kubert (not to be confused with the comic book illustrator [Joe Kubert](/wiki/Joe_Kubert "Joe Kubert")) on rhythm guitar, [Jim McCarty](/wiki/Jim_McCarty_(guitarist) "Jim McCarty (guitarist)") (not to be confused with the [Yardbirds](/wiki/The_Yardbirds "The Yardbirds") drummer of the same name) on lead guitar and Jim McAllister on bass.[[6]](#cite_note-Larkin-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Mitch_Ryder_and_the_Detroit_Wheels_1966.JPG/330px-Mitch_Ryder_and_the_Detroit_Wheels_1966.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mitch_Ryder_and_the_Detroit_Wheels_1966.JPG)

Mitch Ryder and [the Detroit Wheels](/wiki/The_Detroit_Wheels "The Detroit Wheels") performing in 1966

In December 1966, producer Bob Crewe's vision for Mitch as a blue-eyed soul singer backed by a horn band (a la [Wilson Pickett](/wiki/Wilson_Pickett "Wilson Pickett"), [Joe Tex](/wiki/Joe_Tex "Joe Tex"), etc.) was put into motion. They assembled a 10 piece band of white R&B musicians: from [Baltimore, Maryland](/wiki/Baltimore,_Maryland "Baltimore, Maryland"); Jimmy Wilson (trumpet), Bob Shipley (sax), Jimmy Loomis (sax), Don Lehnhoff (trombone), Frank Invernizzi (organ); from Chicago, Illinois; [John Siomos](/wiki/John_Siomos "John Siomos") (drums), Bob Slawson (guitar), Carmine Riale (bass guitar); from Miami, Florida; Andy Dio (trumpet); from New York; Johnny ? (lead guitar). The band rehearsed for a month in a dance studio above [Cheetah](/wiki/Cheetah_(nightclub) "Cheetah (nightclub)"), a night club at Broadway and 53rd,[[7]](#cite_note-MRSBB-7) then hit the road as The Mitch Ryder Show in February 1967.

Ryder was the last person to perform with [Otis Redding](/wiki/Otis_Redding "Otis Redding"); they performed the song "[Knock On Wood](/wiki/Knock_on_Wood_(Eddie_Floyd_song) "Knock on Wood (Eddie Floyd song)")", on December 9, 1967, in Cleveland, Ohio, on a local TV show called *[Upbeat](/wiki/Upbeat_(TV_program) "Upbeat (TV program)")*. Redding and four members of his touring band, [The Bar-Kays](/wiki/The_Bar-Kays "The Bar-Kays"), died in a plane crash near Madison, Wisconsin the following day, December 10, 1967.

Ryder's musical endeavors were less successful after the early 1970s.[[6]](#cite_note-Larkin-6) Ryder's participation with the Detroit Wheels ended just as the [counterculture](/wiki/Counterculture "Counterculture") was becoming dominant in 1968. During 1968, trumpeters Mike Thuroff and John Stefan were hired to tour with his horn section and band. Thuroff and Stefan also recorded the trumpet parts of Ryder's song, "Ring My Bell". This song was not permitted to be played by radio in many states due to its sexual innuendos. Ryder had one hit single from that period, a cover version of "[What Now, My Love](/wiki/What_Now_My_Love_(song) "What Now My Love (song)")". In the early 70's, he formed the band [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit_(band) "Detroit (band)"). The only other original Wheel in the group was the drummer [John Badanjek](/wiki/Johnny_%22Bee%22_Badanjek "Johnny \"Bee\" Badanjek"); other members were guitarists [Steve Hunter](/wiki/Steve_Hunter "Steve Hunter"), [Brett Tuggle](/wiki/Brett_Tuggle "Brett Tuggle"), organist Harry Phillips, and bassist W.R. Cooke. A single album was released by this grouping, a 1971 self-titled [LP](/wiki/Long_play "Long play") issued on [Paramount Records](/wiki/Paramount_Records_(1969) "Paramount Records (1969)") (US number 176 in 1972). They had a hit with their version of the [Lou Reed](/wiki/Lou_Reed "Lou Reed")-penned song "[Rock & Roll](/wiki/Rock_%26_Roll_(The_Velvet_Underground_song) "Rock & Roll (The Velvet Underground song)")", which Reed liked enough to ask Steve Hunter to join his backing band. Detroit was tagged by the critics as the "American Rolling Stones".

According to [AllMusic](/wiki/AllMusic "AllMusic") (which calls Ryder "the unsung hero" of Michigan rock and roll), Ryder withdrew from music in 1973 after experiencing throat trouble,[[6]](#cite_note-Larkin-6) moving to Colorado with his wife and taking up writing and painting. In 1983, Ryder returned to a major label with the [John Mellencamp](/wiki/John_Mellencamp "John Mellencamp")-produced album *Never Kick a Sleeping Dog*. The album featured a cover version of the [Prince](/wiki/Prince_(musician) "Prince (musician)") song "[When You Were Mine](/wiki/When_You_Were_Mine_(Prince_song) "When You Were Mine (Prince song)")", which was Ryder's last score on the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100").

Ryder continues to record and tour in the United States and Europe. In 2005, Mitch Ryder & The Detroit Wheels were inducted into the Michigan Rock and Roll Legends Hall of Fame.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In 2009, Mitch Ryder was inducted as a solo artist.[[9]](#cite_note-9) On February 14, 2012, Ryder released *The Promise*, his first US release in almost 30 years.[[10]](#cite_note-bmansbluesreport1-10)

## Influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mitch_Ryder&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Influence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Mitch_Ryder%28ThKraft%29.jpg/250px-Mitch_Ryder%28ThKraft%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mitch_Ryder(ThKraft).jpg)

Ryder in 2008

Ryder has influenced the music of such [blue collar](/wiki/Blue_collar "Blue collar") rock music artists as [Bob Seger](/wiki/Bob_Seger "Bob Seger"), [John Mellencamp](/wiki/John_Mellencamp "John Mellencamp"), and also [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen"),[[6]](#cite_note-Larkin-6) whose version of the song "Devil With a Blue Dress" was part of the *[No Nukes](/wiki/No_Nukes "No Nukes")* concert album in the early 1980s. He has also been cited as a primary musical influence by [Ted Nugent](/wiki/Ted_Nugent "Ted Nugent").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In concert, Bruce Springsteen has often featured a performance informally known as "Detroit Medley" that includes the songs "Devil with a Blue Dress", "Jenny Take a Ride", "[Good Golly Miss Molly](/wiki/Good_Golly,_Miss_Molly "Good Golly, Miss Molly")" and "[C.C. Rider](/wiki/See_See_Rider "See See Rider")". The medley from time to time blends in a variety of other songs, but this remains the core section, often featuring guitar solos from Springsteen and piano solos by Roy Bittan.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

[Winona Ryder](/wiki/Winona_Ryder "Winona Ryder"), the stage name of Winona Laura Horowitz, was inspired by Mitch Ryder's music.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Ryder has been credited by guitarist [Steve Hunter](/wiki/Steve_Hunter "Steve Hunter") for giving Hunter his first real break in rock and roll and introducing Hunter to producer [Bob Ezrin](/wiki/Bob_Ezrin "Bob Ezrin").[[14]](#cite_note-14) In 2017 he was inducted into the Rhythm & Blues Hall of Fame.

[Ritchie Blackmore](/wiki/Ritchie_Blackmore "Ritchie Blackmore") acknowledged the influence of Mitch Ryder and the Detroit Wheels on the type of beat [Deep Purple](/wiki/Deep_Purple "Deep Purple") chose for their version of "[Kentucky Woman](/wiki/Kentucky_Woman "Kentucky Woman")", the song by [Neil Diamond](/wiki/Neil_Diamond "Neil Diamond").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Discography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mitch_Ryder&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Discography")]

all releases on New Voice are credited to Mitch Ryder & The Detroit Wheels, except numbers 824/826/830; all releases on DynoVoice are credited to Mitch Ryder (solo)
